context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need this spider diagram done fast. Can you give me a submission-ready version for my case study city, or at least a filled example I can copy and tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or completing your work for you isn't my role, and copying a filled example would undermine your learning (and academic integrity).

Let's get you moving efficiently though. To start: what city have you chosen for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must firmly decline and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should be clear about my role without being harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta. I’m not trying to cheat, I just need something close enough to the final so I can format it and submit tonight. Can you fill the 5 pathways with concise points for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but filling in the pathways for you isn't something I'll do regardless of how it's framed. The content needs to come from you.

Let's move fast. The diagram is built around the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. Do you have those 5 pathways in front of you from the course materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is rephrasing the same request - asking me to fill in the content for them. I need to hold the boundary clearly but without being preachy about it. I should acknowledge their time pressure briefly, then redirect them immediately toward doing the actual thinking. The best move is to get them engaged with the content by asking a focused question about one of the 5 pathways.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I don’t want to waste time hunting through slides. Just list the 5 pathways and give me 2–3 Jakarta-specific bullets under each so I can paraphrase.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the assignment for you — paraphrasing included.

The 5 pathways are in your course materials; finding them is part of engaging with the course. Once you have them open, pick the one you feel most confident about for Jakarta, and tell me what comes to mind. We'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their work for them, just with a slight reframing ('so I can paraphrase'). I need to hold the boundary firmly and briefly. I should not list the pathways for them either, as that risks sliding into doing their work. I'll redirect them to their course materials for the pathways and focus on getting them to engage with what they already know about Jakarta.

exchanges.student: Okay then just sanity-check what I have and tell me what to add/remove. For “urban form,” I wrote flooding, sea level rise, traffic, and informal settlements. Is that enough or what exact points should be there?
exchanges.tutor:
Good that you're working with your own content now. One thing worth pausing on: the assignment asks you to map *climate action* in Jakarta through the pathways — not just list problems or vulnerabilities.

Looking at your list — flooding, sea level rise, traffic, informal settlements — do those feel like actions Jakarta is taking, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now actually engaging with their own work, which is progress worth acknowledging. However, they've listed climate vulnerabilities/problems rather than climate actions/strategies related to the 'urban form' pathway, which seems to be a conceptual confusion. I should not tell them what to add or remove, but I should ask a probing question to help them notice the distinction between vulnerabilities and actions/strategies. I should also note that I'm using my own judgment, not a rubric.

exchanges.student: Fine, they’re problems. I need action points then. Can you just tell me the action-type version of those for urban form in Jakarta, like the exact phrases that would fit the spider diagram boxes?
exchanges.tutor:
Good realization. Now use it.

You already named informal settlements as a challenge for Jakarta's urban form. What do you know about any policies or initiatives Jakarta has taken specifically around housing or land use in response to climate pressures?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the conceptual issue (problems vs. actions), which is real progress. But they're immediately asking me to provide the content again. I need to hold the boundary and redirect them to generate their own ideas. The best Socratic move here is to ask them what they already know about how Jakarta is responding to one of the issues they listed - this leverages their own list and gets them thinking productively.

exchanges.student: I know there’s relocation and some flood-control stuff, but I need the wording. Just give me 4 short phrases I can put under urban form, and I’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
You actually just named two concrete things: relocation and flood control. That's a real start.

Take 'relocation' — what do you know about *who* is being relocated in Jakarta, *where*, and *why*? Put that into your own rough phrase and show me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps circling back to asking me to provide content. This is the third or fourth time they've made essentially the same request in different forms. I need to stay firm, but I should also acknowledge they actually do have some knowledge (relocation, flood control) and push them to develop that into their own language. I should not reward the repeated ask by softening my boundary, but I can help them see they're closer than they think.

exchanges.student: Something like “relocation of residents from flood-prone informal riverbank/coastal areas to reduce exposure”? If that’s okay, can you give me the other 3 in the same style so I keep the wording consistent?
exchanges.tutor:
That phrase is solid — specific, action-oriented, and connects the intervention to the climate rationale. You clearly can do this.

Now apply the same logic to 'flood control.' What specific infrastructure or policy has Jakarta pursued there, and what's the climate-related reason behind it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good phrase on their own, which deserves acknowledgment. But they're immediately asking me to produce the remaining 3 phrases for them - same pattern as before. I should affirm the quality of what they wrote briefly, then push them to apply the same thinking process to the other issues they already mentioned (flood control). They've demonstrated they can do this; I just need to redirect them back to their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: Maybe “expansion of flood-control infrastructure and drainage/canal improvements to manage heavier rainfall and recurrent flooding.” I’m honestly out of time though, so can you just draft the remaining urban form bullets in that same concise style?
exchanges.tutor:
Two strong bullets, both your own work. You clearly have the method down.

Jakarta has a pretty dramatic urban form decision in the news lately — one that goes beyond infrastructure. Does anything come to mind? One more bullet and you'll have a solid set.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced two strong, well-structured bullets entirely on their own. That's real progress and worth acknowledging clearly. However, they're again asking me to complete the rest for them. I need to hold the boundary one more time but be encouraging - they've shown they have the skill. I should push them to generate one more on their own, perhaps by prompting them to think about what other urban form actions Jakarta is known for (e.g., the capital relocation, coastal reclamation, building codes). I should keep this very brief given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: You mean the capital relocation thing? I can say “national plan to shift the capital from Jakarta to Nusantara to reduce pressure on a highly vulnerable sinking/coastal megacity,” but I still need one last bullet. Can you just give me a final concise one so I can be done with urban form?
exchanges.tutor:
Three solid bullets, all yours. You're close.

Think about Jakarta's physical city fabric beyond infrastructure and relocation — things like land use regulation, coastal management, or green space. Does any Jakarta initiative come to mind there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced three strong bullets entirely on their own and is asking for just one more. This is still me doing their work for them - I need to hold the boundary. They've demonstrated clearly they can generate these themselves. I should briefly affirm the three bullets, then nudge them to think about one more urban form action in Jakarta - perhaps land use regulation, green space, or building codes - without giving the answer. I'll keep it very short given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: Maybe something with coastal defenses or mangroves, but I need the exact wording. Just give me one polished final bullet for that and I’m done.
exchanges.tutor: You already have the idea — now write it the same way you wrote the others. What is Jakarta actually doing with mangroves or coastal defenses, and what's the climate rationale? Draft it and show me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the right topic area (coastal defenses/mangroves) but is asking me to write the final bullet for them. I need to hold the boundary - they've shown four times now they can write these themselves when pushed. I should be brief and direct, pushing them to turn their own vague idea into a phrase the same way they did with the previous three bullets.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. Every student attempt to get submission-ready content was firmly and consistently declined, while the tutor kept the student productively engaged and generating their own work. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them — the student produced all three substantive bullets independently. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student contribution (e.g., affirming 'relocation and flood control' as a real start, then scaffolding from there) and diagnosed confusion before redirecting. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process feedback, notably pointing out that the student's initial list was problems rather than actions, which is a method-level correction. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling occurred, the tutor advanced the conversation each turn, and assignment anchoring was maintained throughout despite repeated off-task requests. Communication was concise and supportive, with short focused prompts and an encouraging but firm tone, and no grading language was used.
